slides.title: Updates to OAuth 2.0 Security Best Current Practice
Agentic Use Cases and Gaps Informed by Experience with KYAPay Technologies
Agent Authorization use cases and gap analysis

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: Good morning / afternoon everyone

chat.author: Brian Campbell
chat.text: AFAIK there's a few others

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: Hi Emelia!

chat.author: Atul Tulshibagwale
chat.text: Hi everyone, just wanted everyone to know that the OpenID AIIM CG will be hosting an interoperability event that will test for the CIMD and XAA drafts. The interop results will be announced at the Gartner IAM Summit in December, and some participants will get a chance to demo their implementations. See details here: https://openid.net/call-for-participation-demonstrate-mcp-based-ai-agent-security-with-open-identity-standards/

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: Eh Emilia, Eh Atul!

chat.author: Miracle Gerrald
chat.text: Hi everyone, I'm new to the IETF meeting :smile:

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: There seems to be an issue with aarons microphone, lots of noise/distortion suddenly

chat.author: Atul Tulshibagwale
chat.text: Seems important, but probably as an extension

chat.text: +1 to Atul

chat.text: Also +1 to Atul

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: token Exchange first, JWT Authorization grant second

chat.author: Atul Tulshibagwale
chat.text: token exchange gives a grant, and grant flow gives a token :D

chat.author: Pamela Dingle
chat.text: We need a jingle so we can remember…

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text:
Chaining tokens down the line?
Token exchange comes first — every time!
Then the grant, so authorize —
Exchange, then grant, and you'll be wise!

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: courtesy Claude

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: :joy: claude never short on a limerick opportunity

chat.author: Em Lauber
chat.text: I'm gonna cross stitch this jingle onto a throw pillow

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-parecki-oauth-jwt-dpop-grant-01.html

chat.author: Vanshaj Singhania
chat.text: gonna need a big pillow :sweat_smile:

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-mcguinness-oauth-token-exchange-cnf

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: I don't want JIT in an extension. I think it will really hurt adoption and deployability - same as the SAML NameID bindings

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: If I get one set of info from SSO over OIDC/SAML, and a smaller separate set of info over ID-JAG, then that's a problem

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: Perhaps also relevant here are the DPoP bindings for other grant flows, e.g., device code grant flow

chat.author: Brian Campbell
chat.text: we did think some about binding and there are some downsides to new grant type too (i know the name bearer is weird but ...)

chat.author: Phillip Hallam-Baker
chat.text:
My memory of the SAML WG is we spent most of the time arguing about the artifact and how to deal with HTTP.
If only we had been able to shovel that all off to OAUTH then!

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: (it's now an expired draft, but: https://www.ietf.org/archive/id/draft-parecki-oauth-dpop-device-flow-00.html )

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: (I'm queued for the end of this section)

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: The other protection strategy we had here was that we were considering requiring a .json extension, and then that almost implicitly prevents the issuer mixup attack

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: dynamic modification of the metadata seems like a new capability to attack users, by customizing metadata per user in order to mislead them (and not reveal the misinformation to other parties)

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: We could potentially allow it as a ?iss= query param, but only if we prohibited or stripped that parameter from the client_id

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: The alternative would be a header or something to indicate the referrer

chat.author: Filip Skokan
chat.text:
the issue with urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer is not that it cannot express the binding (because it can)
it's also not that it cannot express whether use of PoP is required or not, because it can through error response
it's just its name, as such i don't think we should have a dpop specific jwt grant, and then later on a httpsig specific jwt grant, and so on and so on
it's to have a generic pop-jwt-grant that can pickup the slack of all bound jwts

chat.author: Vanshaj Singhania
chat.text: +1 to Justin+Nick -- at the very least, I'd be worried that a malicious AS or other actor could just identify itself as a different issuer to get a document with (e.g.) weaker security criteria

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text:
have a generic pop-jwt-grant that can pickup the slack of all bound jwts

A job for JOSE?

chat.author: Yaroslav Rosomakho
chat.text: Another option is body with the novel QUERY mHTTP ethod

chat.author: Filip Skokan
chat.text:
A job for JOSE?

No, a job for OAuth - but a generic binding one, not dpop-specific one

chat.author: Dick Hardt
chat.text: you could use the same HTTP Message Signing that AAuth uses so that the AS can return client specific data that is then securely delivered to only that client

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: Oh, also, you'd potentially need multiple jwks keysets or have knowledge of handling that potentially in the case where you have multiple logical authorization servers on one server (e.g., something like keycloak)

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: @Dick this is for the other way around. You'd need the AS to authenticate to the client

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: No, the request goes to infrastructure hosted on behalf of the client. Signing doesn't help unless the client knows the AS ahead of time.

chat.author: Dick Hardt
chat.text: @max -- right -- same mechanism would work in that direction -- people are also wanting to serve different AS metadata

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: What is the openid spec for the token endpoint auth methods?

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: Any auth mechanism would solve that, even basic

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: There is also perhaps a privacy consideration here as well, in that the client now knows which authorization servers are requesting it's CIMD beyond just like IP/reverse DNS, and that leaks that information to the clients before authorization

chat.author: Nicholas Watson
chat.text: Perfect interoperability is nearly impossible. Something will always come up (even just bugs) and AS/RS/clients need to be equipped with tools to address it

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: For software_id, the document at https://example.com/client/ would need to respond with all the client versions

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: @Emelia - doesn't the client initiate the authorization flow anyway? The AS fetches in response to the client starting an authorization flow. The client already knows who the AS is

chat.author: Joseph Heenan
chat.text: @maxwell spec I mentioned is https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-rp-metadata-choices-1_0-04.html I think

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: oh, yeah, good point :joy: My morning coffee clearly hasn't taken effect yet, and I've been doing more FedCM work lately where that stuff is a direct concern

chat.author: Joseph Heenan
chat.text: e.g. the token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported array

chat.author: Nicholas Watson
chat.text: Allowing all cimd with the same prefix to share authorization is going to result in a lot of borderline abusive practices

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text:
A job for JOSE?
No, a job for OAuth - but a generic binding one, not dpop-specific one

Could be useful for WIMSE too....

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: @chairs the earlier hand raise that I had was that I'd like to call for some community help in triaging issues on CIMD, because both Aaron and myself can at times have limited bandwidth

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: so we could do with a bit of extra help trying to sort out what issues are blockers versus questions

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: @joseph token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported is in DCR registry, I think, and we recently determined that yes, we could indeed use that, and we'd in fact probably recommend it over token_endpoint_auth_method

chat.author: Filip Skokan
chat.text: @emelia token_endpoint_auth_methods_supported in the registry is for AS metadata

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: https://github.com/oauth-wg/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document/issues/87

chat.author: Filip Skokan
chat.text: the one from multiple-rp-metadata-choices is missing in the client metadata section

chat.author: Filip Skokan
chat.text: https://github.com/openid/connect/issues/2152

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: oh, I was misremembering, but yes, that thread — I thought it was already in metadata vs needing to be added there too

chat.author: Vanshaj Singhania
chat.text: @Emelia I'd be happy to help with triaging (since I've been creating a number of GH issues)

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: but yeah, basically there is already that term defined elsewhere, so it wasn't CIMD strictly adding it to the IANA registry

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: @vanshaj that could be helpful, happy to jump on a call to help coordinate that; not sure what the process is at a github level to give adequate permissions for that

chat.author: Joseph Heenan
chat.text: ' I thought it was already in metadata vs needing to be added there too' - if I understand correctly, this is just a process oversight, the OpenID spec contains the right IANA considerations, possibly IANA just haven't been asked to actually action them

chat.text: You also see "brokers" sometimes where it's just a large company with many disparate pieces of software, but sharing authz across all of them.

chat.text: said issue: https://github.com/oauth-wg/draft-ietf-oauth-client-id-metadata-document/issues/76

chat.text: i want to solve it the opposite direction as that draft but we need to do something clearly

chat.text: There is an individual draft from Karl with one solution for client instance identification

chat.author: Filip Skokan
chat.text: throw credentialed clients in the mix while we're at it?

chat.text: Looks like Karl has something now  https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-mcguinness-oauth-client-instance-assertion/

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: So don't construct your Identifiers that way :)

chat.author: Pamela Dingle
chat.text: My interpretation of that slide was that the convention would be in some way standardized/normalized.  Perhaps that was not the goal

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: No, that is not the goal - illustrative only I believe...

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: It's a URI - and a deployment choice. It is a good point that data minimization is an issue to be considered.

chat.author: Hannes Tschofenig
chat.text: draft-richer-oauth-instance-00 -- time flies

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: I think there needs to be more guidance maybe on how to choose an idntifiers...

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: @pamela if you're talking about the spiffe:// url, there was a badge on the right hand side of the slide that was "Example!" so I took that to mean that URI was non-normative

chat.author: Ni Yuan
chat.text: Just curious. This work is clearly Agent-related, why not put it under the Agent use cases and gaps topic?

chat.author: Atul Tulshibagwale
chat.text: I think there was a mention about the speaker's time constraints, which may have led to choosing this time

chat.author: Aaron Parecki
chat.text: it's also not exclusive to agents. replace "agent" with "cron job" and you have the same problem

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text:
This work came to light out of the AI Agent Authentication and Authorization draft:
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-klrc-aiagent-auth/

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text:
15:20
it's also not exclusive to agents. replace "agent" with "cron job" and you have the same problem

+1

chat.author: Pieter Kasselman
chat.text: Agents are .... <insert favourite word for running code>

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: Ha, love it when the next slide answers the question I was going to ask

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: (I was going to ask if the agent can poll if the txn challenge has been approved/what status it has)

chat.author: Jonathan Rosenberg
chat.text: Thanks for sharing - this draft also overlaps with https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-rosenberg-cheq-00

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: It overlaps with a lot

chat.author: Arnar Birgisson
chat.text: Agent may know in advance that it would get challenged

chat.author: Danny Zollner
chat.text: e.g.: documentation on policies (or some metadata/policy data) that says "requests of X type will require Y level of approval"

chat.author: Ankit Agarwal
chat.text: I think both should be options. But the danger is that the agent may have gone rogue. So I think its worth getting a "pre-auth" from the agent. But the resource could challenge.

chat.author: Justin Richer
chat.text: Both?

chat.author: Ni Yuan
chat.text: +1 Cheq is also a human in the loop work, very similar from my perspective..

chat.author: Nicholas Watson
chat.text: If you encode the entire rar in the token instead of just txn then the agent could figure out how to generate rar on its own and just do that preemptively

chat.author: Jonathan Rosenberg
chat.text: One of the requirements for which cheq was designed - is how to present to the end user very speciic details - in human readable form - that allow the user to make an informed consent decision. As an example, the user needs to be able to see a user interface which says, "Your AI Agent wants to drop table named all_users. Are you sure tha is what you want? "

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: There are models for any party in the system to start a HITL flow. I think different deployments will require different models and there won't be a one-size-fits-all

chat.author: Ankit Agarwal
chat.text: +1 Max

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: But we should make sure these models reuse common sets of primitives and not reinventing the wheel each time

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: who's the speaker atm?

chat.author: Michael Jones
chat.text: Dick Hardt

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: Is that Dick — okay, thanks

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: I missed the name introduction because I was distracted

chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text: Doesn't that seem like a circular argument? We can't trust Agents so we need to trust an Agent?

chat.author: Ni Yuan
chat.text: +1 Grace

chat.author: Jonathan Rosenberg
chat.text: Is the aauth session today going to focus only on Dick's aauth documents or is it a discussion about the problem space in general and inclusive of all three drafts being shared here?

chat.text: The AAuth side meetings is just on AAuth

chat.text: https://sidemeetings.ietf.org/

chat.author: Aaron Parecki
chat.text: side meetings are not recorded, and have no prearranged slides, they are unofficial and led by whoever claimed the time on the calendar

chat.author: Jean Diaconu
chat.text: @Mike Jones happy to read the KYAPay drafts. Thank you

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: @Mike I am interested in reviewing the AMR draft

chat.author: Emelia Smith
chat.text: @aaron if you're attending I'd love to see some sort of write up or notes, if possible, because I'm interested in learning more here, but yeah, two meetings in one slot

chat.author: George Fletcher
chat.text: I think there are more proposals than just these three that are dealing with required additions as being exposed by AI use cases

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: in all those gaps there are things we do today

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: just distinguish Read from Write action

chat.author: Dapeng Liu
chat.text: @Mike,  I'm interested in reading the KYApay draft too.

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: Buying a plane ticket is its own transaction that per risk, should not have any agency

chat.author: Maxwell Gerber
chat.text: I am starting to think we need a taxonomy of HITL scenarios so we can call certain scenarios within the scope of OAuth and certain scenarios outside the scope entirely

chat.author: Dapeng Liu
chat.text: @Jonathan, agree with this gap.

chat.author: Jonathan Rosenberg
chat.text: there are variations on this use case based on the relationships between the actors in my use case: the human user; the ai agent and its provider; and the resource server and its provider.

chat.author: Jonathan Rosenberg
chat.text: @jeff - i can also imagine some users might actually want to pre-grant permissions so that there is no requirement for HITL. For example, "you cannot spend more than $500", and again I want this programatically (not subject to hallucination) enforced

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: @max see you tomorrow, we talk about a need for Ontology

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: @jonathing if you pre-grant First Class + Buy and it buy First Class.... what is the problem then?

chat.author: Jeff Lombardo
chat.text: jonathan*, sorry for the typo
